actions.thought: Hand 1 of the tournament and I am on the button with King-Trey offsuit. In a three-handed game, the button should open a wide range, but K3o is at the very bottom of that range and is often a fold in standard GTO play. Given the 100bb stack depth, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and observe the dynamics of the opponents rather than entering a pot with a weak hand that has poor playability post-flop.

With pocket sixes in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong hand for a blind versus blind situation. Raising to 300 (3bb) is standard to deny equity, take control of the hand, and potentially win the pot right now. If called, I can continuation bet favorable flops or try to flop a set.

Q2 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in No-Limit Hold'em. With no positional advantage and a raise from the small blind, there is no strategic justification for calling. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the Button with 8s2h, which is one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em. With 8 high and no suitedness or connectivity, I have virtually no playability post-flop. I will just fold and wait for a better spot to attack the blinds.

With 5c 6s offsuit in the small blind, I am facing a heads-up situation against the big blind. While the hand is weak, completing the blind to see a flop is mathematically justified given the pot odds, as I am only needing to call 50 chips to contest a 150-chip pot.

Flashlight limped from the small blind, giving me the option to see a free flop. While Ace-Trey offsuit has decent high-card value, it is not strong enough to warrant a raise for value or as a bluff in this spot early in the tournament. Checking is the most prudent play to see the flop without inflating the pot out of position.

I have bottom pair on a paired board (Jack high). With a weak kicker and the board texture, my hand has very little showdown value against any Jack or overpair. Betting would be overplaying a weak hand. I will check to see how the opponent acts, planning to fold if they show significant aggression.

The board is Jd-Js-5h, which is a paired and very dry board. I have Ace-high with As 3h. Flashlight checked to me. Since the pot is small and Ace-high has some showdown value against a limped range, there is no need to bet and risk a check-raise or turn a showdown hand into a bluff. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free and potentially improve or realize my equity without inflating the pot.

On the turn, the board has paired with the 5d, completing my hand into a full house (Fives full of Jacks). Since we checked the flop, the likelihood of the opponent holding a Jack is reduced, as a Jack would typically be betting for value. I am now in a very strong position and should look to extract value from any Jack-high or smaller pair hand the opponent might be holding, or simply establish a lead to force a decision. A bet of 100, which is half the pot, is a solid value bet that can be called by weaker hands while allowing me to evaluate if the opponent raises, which would signal a potential Jack in their hand.

The board has double-paired with Jacks and Fives. Currently, my Ace-high (As 3h) plays as Jacks and Fives with an Ace kicker, which is the best possible non-boat/quads hand. In a limped pot where the flop was checked through, Flashlight is unlikely to have a Jack, though a Five is possible. However, Flashlight could also be betting with a worse kicker or bluffing on a dry board. Calling is the best option to see the river and reach showdown, as my Ace kicker provides significant value and I am getting 3:1 odds on a call.

With a full house (Fives full of Jacks), I hold a very strong hand, essentially the second nuts. The only hand that currently beats me is pocket Jacks or a single Jack (quads). Given my opponent called a turn bet, they likely hold a Jack or a mid-pair, which they may still call with on the river. A value bet of 200 (half the pot) is appropriate to extract value from those weaker holdings while not over-committing against a potential quad J scenario.

On this double-paired board of Jacks and Fives (Jd Js 5h 5d 4c), my Ace-high (As 3h) constitutes a two-pair hand (Jacks and Fives) with an Ace kicker. This is the best possible hand that does not involve a Jack or a Five. In a limped pot that was checked through the flop, it is statistically unlikely for Flashlight to have a Jack, and only one Five remains in the deck. Flashlight's betting on the turn and river could represent a small pocket pair, an attempt to buy the pot on a dry board, or another Ace (resulting in a split). Given the 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 800), my hand is far too strong to fold as a bluff-catcher.

Ks-2s is a marginal hand, especially with a weak kicker. While being on the button allows for more flexibility, opening with this hand carries significant risk of being dominated by better kings or falling behind to aggressive play from the blinds. Given the long tournament structure, I prefer to conserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind with J5s after the Button has folded. In a blind-versus-blind situation, J5s is a strong enough hand to play aggressively. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. If the Big Blind calls, I have a suited hand with some connectivity that plays reasonably well post-flop, even though I will be out of position. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate.

Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand that faces reverse implied odds when called against a 3x open from the Small Blind. Despite having position post-flop, it's safer to fold and look for a better spot early in the tournament to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with 9d 6c. While it is a 3-handed game and positional play is important, 96 offsuit is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening range, even from the button. Given the stacks are deep and I have no specific reads suggesting the blinds are over-folding, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better opportunity to involve myself in a pot.

93o is an extremely weak hand. Although it is folded to me in the small blind, raising as a steal with a hand this disconnected and weak is generally unprofitable against the big blind who will have position post-flop. The standard play is to fold.

Jack-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Even with the positional advantage of being on the Button, trying to steal with this hand against two players is a losing proposition in the long run. The best play is to fold and wait for better opportunities.

With [2h, Ts] in the small blind, my hand is significantly below the threshold for calling or raising against the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined strategic decision to preserve my stack for stronger opportunities.

I am on the button with Ten of Diamonds and Trey of Diamonds. This is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game, even for a button open. Limping into the pot is generally a poor strategy, and with no initiative, folding is the most disciplined and effective play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Q9o. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind. Q9o is a relatively strong hand in a head-up blind battle. I will raise to 300 to either win the blinds immediately or play the hand with initiative and a range advantage.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand against a Small Blind open. By flat-calling, I keep the SB's opening range wide, retaining the weaker Aces and broadway cards that my hand dominates, while also guaranteeing I play the rest of the hand in position. 3-betting might isolate me against better hands like AJ+ or premium pairs.

I have top pair (Queens) with an open-ended straight draw (9-T-J-Q) on a very coordinated board. I was the pre-flop aggressor. I will place a continuation bet of half the pot to extract value from drawing hands and weaker pairs, while also protecting my hand against potential overcards or completed draws on the turn.

Facing a half-pot continuation bet on a very wet and coordinated T-J-Q board, my hand holds bottom pair (Tens), an Ace overcard, and a gutshot draw to the nut straight (any King). Having the Ace also serves as a strong blocker to opponent's value hands like AQ, AJ, and AK. Given the pot odds and positional advantage, this is a reasonable spot to float. I have enough equity to call and evaluate the turn, whether I improve to a straight/two pair or my opponent slows down.

On the turn, the 3rd card of clubs appears, which is largely a brick. I still hold top pair (Queens) with an open-ended straight draw (8s and Kings). While the board is quite dangerous with many two-pair and straight possibilities in the Big Blind's range, I need to continue betting for value against hands like AJ, KJ, or lower pairs and draws that might have called the flop. I will bet 600, which is half the pot, to maintain initiative and protect my hand.

Facing a double barrel on a highly coordinated T-Q-J-3 board, my third pair (Tens) and 4-out gutshot straight draw are extremely weak. Flash's betting range likely consists of stronger made hands such as straights, sets, two pair, or higher pairs. Even against semi-bluffs, my equity is marginal at best. The pot odds do not justify calling to chase a gutshot, making folding the only sound decision.
I am on the button with Ac 6c, a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise of 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds.
Queen-six offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button, folding from the small blind is the only sensible and standard play to conserve stack size for better opportunities.

I am in the big blind holding 3c-9h, which is a very weak starting hand with poor equity. Calling an open raise from the button (Flash) with this hand would require me to play out of position against a range that is likely wider but still significantly stronger than mine. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
I have 7-2 offsuit on the button, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Folding is the only sensible choice here.

With the button folding, I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind. While Q7o is a marginal hand, in a blind-versus-blind situation, it is often profitable to raise to steal the pot rather than folding or limping. I will raise to 250 to apply pressure and attempt to win the blinds without seeing a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 2h, which is a very weak starting hand. Flashlight raised from the Small Blind to 250. Although I would have post-flop position against the Small Blind, 52o is too weak to defend even with the pot odds provided. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more playable hand.

I am in the button position with 6d-Th, which is a weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. It does not meet the requirements for an opening raise or a call against the blinds in a three-handed game. Folding is the standard professional play here to avoid playing a negative expected value (-EV) pot.
K9o is a solid hand for a blind-vs-blind confrontation. After the Button folds, I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the hand.

Opponent Flash opens to 3x from the Small Blind. While I have position post-flop, T9o is a marginal offsuit connector that is often dominated by their stealing/value range (like Ax, Kx, Qx, higher connectors). At a 3x sizing, the pot odds aren't compelling enough to call with this hand. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

In a three-handed game, the button should generally play a wide range; however, Queen-Six offsuit is near the bottom of a standard opening range. Given my high activity and successful steals in previous hands, I will fold this marginal hand to maintain a credible image and avoid playing a weak hand out of position if one of the blinds decides to defend or three-bet.
9s 3s is a very weak hand, even suited, and I am out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9h4s in a 3-handed game. This is a very weak, offsuit, disconnected hand that plays poorly post-flop and has essentially no equity against standard calling ranges. The standard play here is an easy fold.

With Ace-Jack offsuit in a heads-up situation against the big blind, I have a strong hand that dominates a wide range of hands the big blind might defend with. Raising to 300 is a standard size that allows me to build a pot, maintain initiative, and potentially take down the blinds immediately. This aggressive play aligns with my strategy to capitalize on premium high-card holdings.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Small Blind. I will 3-bet to 900 for value and to take the initiative, leveraging my positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. Facing a 3-bet from the big blind, I am out of position, making a 4-bet risky as it may commit me to a large pot with a marginal holding against a strong range. Calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable and evaluate the flop, leveraging the strength of my Ace-high hand without over-committing.

The flop is King-high, which misses my Ace-Jack hand entirely. Since the opponent 3-bet pre-flop, they likely have a strong range that often connects well with this board. Checking is the most strategic play as it allows me to control the pot size and avoid committing more chips without any pair or draw, while keeping my options open to potentially fold to a significant continuation bet or check-fold later.

I have Ace-high with the Ace of spades on a King-high board. As the pre-flop 3-better, this board is much better for my perceived range (which includes AK, KQ, and AA/KK/QQ) than for Flashlight's calling range. A continuation bet of 600 (1/3 pot) allows me to maintain initiative, pressure his weaker holdings like mid-pairs or suited connectors that missed, and potentially take down the pot right now. If called, I still have overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw.

My hand is Ace-Jack high on a King-high board, which provides no improvement. Given that the opponent 3-bet pre-flop and is now betting on the flop, their range heavily contains Kings, pocket pairs, or better. Calling with Ace-high out of position against a strong 3-betting range is a negative expected value play. I will fold and save my chips for a more favorable situation.

My hand, Jack-Six offsuit, is not a playable starting hand in this position. Even with three players at the table, opening with such a weak hand from the button is non-profitable. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

In a blind-versus-blind scenario, King-Seven suited is a premium starting hand. Given that the button has folded, I am only competing against the Big Blind. Raising here is standard to either win the pot immediately or build a pot with a strong hand while in position for most of the rest of the hand (though technically out of position post-flop, the strength of the hand warrants an aggressive approach). Pro has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in several previous hands, so a standard 3x raise to 300 is optimal.
I have T3o in the big blind facing a 3x raise from the small blind. This hand is incredibly weak and offers very little playability post-flop. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While I have been aggressive in late positions in previous hands, J6o is a marginal hand that sits just outside a standard button opening range in a 3-handed game. Given my chip lead and the fact that I've been raising frequently, folding here helps maintain a balanced image and avoids playing a weak hand against the blinds who may start defending more aggressively. I will wait for a stronger hand to continue my pressure.

With 6d 9h in the small blind and the button folding, I have a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a typical big blind calling range. Raising as a steal with this specific hand is too loose, and calling is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play.

J8 offsuit is a weak hand to play, even from the button. Given the aggressive tendencies of the players in the blinds (especially Flash), attempting a steal with a marginal holding like this is likely to get us into trouble. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve our stack.

I am in the small blind position, and the button has folded. This is a blind-vs-blind situation, which allows for a wider range of starting hands. While 6-J offsuit is not a premium hand, it is playable in this context to see a flop, and completing the small blind is a standard strategic move to minimize risk while maintaining position to the big blind.

I hold a pocket pair of fives in the Big Blind. Flashlight in the Small Blind has limped in. In a three-handed game, pocket fives are relatively strong, and I have positional advantage over the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand. Raising here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or build a pot where I am likely ahead of a limping range while having the benefit of acting last on subsequent streets.

Jack-Six offsuit is a weak, marginal hand, especially when playing out of position in the small blind. The big blind has shown aggression by raising to 300. Defending with this holding is not mathematically sound or strategically advisable given the lack of equity and the disadvantage of being out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my stack.

I am in the button position holding Queen-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with little to no equity. Playing this hand would be reckless, even in a three-handed game. Given my stack size and position, the most disciplined and profitable play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Kc4s. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). I have been raising frequently in this position and the Big Blind has shown a tendency to fold to these steals in previous hands (Hands 3, 7, 9, 12). Although K4o is a relatively weak starting hand, its high-card value makes it a strong candidate for a blind-vs-blind steal. I will maintain my aggressive image and raise to 300 to attempt to take down the pot pre-flop.
7d 3c is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, there is no reason to defend or get involved. It's a clear fold.

I have A8o on the button, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I've been consistently raising to 300 from this position to steal the blinds or build a pot with range advantage. Both opponents have been relatively fold-prone to my button raises recently, so I will continue with this aggressive strategy.
I am dealt an offsuit 7-5, which is a very weak hand. I am facing a 3x open raise from the Button while in the Small Blind. Out of position with a junk hand, folding is the only correct play.

King-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the big blind against a button open, especially when playing out of position. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting, the hand is frequently dominated by the opponent's opening range, and it will be difficult to navigate the board post-flop. With plenty of tournament life remaining, there is no need to engage in marginal spots. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

Ten-Nine offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game is a marginal hand. Given that Flash in the Big Blind has shown a very aggressive tendency in previous hands, opening with a marginal offsuit connector invites unnecessary difficult post-flop spots or forces us to fold to a 3-bet. Folding is the most prudent and safe play here.

I am in the small blind with K-2 offsuit, and the button (Pro) has folded, leaving a heads-up situation against the big blind. While K-2 is not a strong hand, it is standard in a blind-vs-blind scenario to defend my position rather than folding, as I have already invested 50 chips. I will call to complete the big blind and see a flop, hoping to catch a favorable board.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 3c. Flashlight limped from the Small Blind. Given that I have a speculative suited hand and can see the flop for free, checking is the most prudent play to minimize risk and see if I can hit a strong hand on the flop without bloating the pot.

I am in the small blind with King-high and no pair or draw on a 6d-8h-Jh board. With a small pot of 200 and no equity, betting would be bluffing into the big blind's checking range, which is risky. Checking is the standard play to minimize losses, control the pot size, and potentially realize equity if a King appears on the turn.

I have 5-high and no significant draws on a board of 6-8-J. Flashlight limped pre-flop and checked the flop. Since I have zero equity and this is a limped pot, there is no need to bluff or build the pot. I will check back and take a free turn card.

I am in a heads-up pot on a board of 6d-8h-Jh-Qd with King-high. My hand has very little equity, as I have no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor diamond draw. Betting here would be a pure bluff into the big blind, which is unlikely to succeed. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach a showdown cheaply or re-evaluate on the river.

I have 5-high with no draws on a board of 6-8-J-Q. Flashlight limped pre-flop and has checked through the flop and turn. While Flashlight's checks indicate weakness, I have zero showdown value and no equity to improve. In a small limped pot, there is little incentive to bluff when I can take a free river card. I will check back.

The board is 6d-8h-Jh-Qd-Qc and I hold Kc-2d for King-high. My opponent has checked through both the flop and turn, indicating they likely don't have a strong hand, but they could easily hold any pair of 6s, 8s, Js, or Qs. Betting as a bluff on the river is unlikely to succeed against a range that checked the turn, and I have zero showdown value. Checking is the most sensible play to see their cards and minimize risk.

I have 5-high with no showdown value. The board is 6-8-J-Q-Q and has been checked through on all streets. While Flashlight's repeated checking suggests weakness, I have zero equity and even the weakest hands in Flashlight's range (like Ace-high or small pairs) will likely call a small bet in this tiny pot. Since I have no investment in this pot beyond the big blind, I will check and concede the hand to avoid losing more chips on a low-frequency bluff.

In a 3-handed game, the button is a powerful position, and I should maintain aggressive pressure. While Ace-three offsuit is a marginal hand, its high-card value combined with positional advantage makes it a suitable candidate to open the pot. Raising to 300 continues my established strategy, aiming to steal the blinds or gain the initiative in the hand.

I have been dealt pocket Queens, a premium hand. Flashlight opened from the button for 300. I am in the small blind, which is an out-of-position post-flop. I should 3-bet here to build value, protect my hand, and simplify the decision-making for the rest of the hand. A raise to 1,000 is a standard size that forces Flashlight to make a difficult decision and likely folds out the Big Blind.

Facing a button open and a large 3-bet from the small blind, calling with pocket sixes is mathematically unprofitable. I would be investing over 10% of my stack set-mining out of position, and I don't even close the action, meaning the original raiser could still 4-bet. The risk is too high and the implied odds are too low to justify continuing.

Ace-three offsuit is a relatively weak hand, especially against a 3-bet. While I am in position, calling a 3-bet with this range is generally negative expected value, as I will frequently be dominated by better Ax hands and struggle to navigate post-flop play. Given my stack size and the current dynamics, the most disciplined and strategic move is to fold and wait for a higher-equity situation.

I am on the button with Td 8h, which is a reasonable hand to open in a 3-handed game. My opponents, Pro and Flashlight, have shown a tendency to fold to 3x raises from the blinds and button in previous hands. I will maintain my aggressive image and open to 300 to try and take down the blinds or play the hand in position.

Facing a 3x open from an aggressive Button. J7 suited is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially with the Big Blind still to act behind me. Calling or 3-betting here carries a negative expected value against typical ranges, so folding is the optimal and safest decision.

Flash is opening from the button, which is a wide range. Holding Ace-Three suited in the big blind, I have sufficient equity to defend. Given the pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win 450 total), calling is the mathematically sound and standard approach to play in position post-flop.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a board that does not hit my range significantly. With only an Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw, checking is the standard play to keep the pot controlled and re-evaluate on the turn after seeing how my opponent acts.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a board that is relatively safe, though it does have a diamond draw. Flashlight checked to me, which is a standard play for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. I will bet for value and to protect my hand against overcards and potential draws. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot should serve this purpose well.

The board is relatively dry (6c 8d 2d). I hold the Ac 3c, which gives me a backdoor nut flush draw and two overcards. Given the pot odds (getting 1050:400) and my relative hand strength, calling is the optimal play to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn has brought the Jack of diamonds, which completes a flush draw on the board. My hand, containing the Ace of clubs, gives me the nut flush draw. Being out of position against the button, checking is the optimal play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on my opponent's action. This allows me to potentially realize my equity for free if the opponent checks back or call if their bet sizing is favorable.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds completes a potential flush and is also an overcard to my pair of eights. While I hold the Ten of Diamonds as a blocker to the flush, my pair of eights has marginal value here. Flashlight's check on the turn could be a trap with a flush or a Jack, or it could be a weak hand looking to see a cheap showdown. By checking back, I control the pot size and realize my showdown equity without risking a check-raise. I can evaluate the river based on Flashlight's next move.

I have Ace-high with a pair of sixes on the board. The opponent checked the turn, suggesting a lack of a strong hand or a desire to control the pot. Given my weak showdown value and the board texture, betting serves no purpose as it will not induce calls from worse hands. Checking is the most logical path to reach showdown and minimize potential losses.

I have two pair, Eights and Sixes, with a Ten kicker. The board is somewhat coordinated with a possible flush (three diamonds) and a Jack as an overcard. Flashlight has checked every street since calling my flop bet. This usually indicates a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to build a bigger pot, or a missed draw. While I might get value from a smaller pocket pair or a weaker Eight, checking back is the safest play to ensure I reach showdown and win the pot without risking a check-raise from a slow-played monster or a better hand like a Jack or a Six.
A9 offsuit is a solid hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to put pressure on the blinds, steal the dead money, and leverage my position post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with J4s. While the pot odds offer 2:1 on a call, playing a weak off-suit connector (or even suited) out of position against a button raise is generally suboptimal, especially with the Big Blind still to act behind me. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot to defend or 3-bet.

I am in the big blind with 9c5c, which is a decent suited connector-style hand. The 'Pro' player on the button has raised to 250, making it 150 more for me to call into a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of roughly 2.6:1. Given the stack depths and the fact that I have a hand with post-flop playability and potential to make flushes or straights, calling is a reasonable defense against a button open, especially since the small blind has already folded.

On a connected flop of 6c-7d-4h, I have a gutshot straight draw (to an 8) and a backdoor flush draw. As the Big Blind defender against a button open, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react. Leading out here doesn't provide much information and I'd prefer to see a cheap turn or evaluate a bet size from 'Pro'.

The 6-7-4 board heavily favors the Big Blind's preflop calling range, as it connects with many suited connectors, one-gappers, and small pocket pairs. Having only Ace-high with no backdoor flush draw, a continuation bet is likely to get called or raised by better hands. Checking back allows me to pot control, realize my equity, and potentially improve on the turn.

On the turn Jack of Diamonds, I still have a gutshot straight draw to an eight. Since 'Pro' checked back on the flop (6-7-4), their range is likely capped or contains marginal showdown value like weak pairs or Ace-high. I will check here to see the river for free, as I have no showdown value but decent equity to improve. If 'Pro' checks behind again, I can re-evaluate on the river.

Flash has checked twice, which often indicates a weak pair, a draw, or air. While a delayed continuation bet on the Jack could potentially fold out hands like King-high or very weak pairs, my Ace-high still retains decent showdown value. Betting here might only get called by better hands (such as any pair or strong draw) while folding out the hands I already beat. By checking, I control the size of the pot, keep his bluffing range wide for the river, and realize my equity for free, allowing me to potentially win at showdown or improve.

I have 9-high on a board of 6-7-4-J-T. I missed my gutshot draw to a straight. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they have a weak hand or marginal showdown value like Ace-high or a small pair. Since I have no showdown value and cannot win by checking, I will bet 300 into the 550 pot to represent a hand like a Jack, a Ten, or a slow-played straight. This bet size should be enough to force a fold from most of Pro's range that hasn't improved.

With Ace-high and no pair on a 6-7-4-J-T board, my hand has very little showdown value against a river bet. Flash's line of check-check-bet on the river could be a value bet with a pair of Tens, Jacks, or a straight (like 8-9), or a bluff with a missed draw. While he might be bluffing a missed diamond draw or a smaller straight draw, calling with just Ace-high is too marginal and unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am on the button with Jack-four of clubs. This is a marginal hand, but in a three-handed game, being on the button provides a significant positional advantage. I want to maintain the initiative and potentially win the blinds, so I will raise to 300. This is a standard opening size that balances aggression with stack conservation.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 2h, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened with a standard raise to 3x from the button. Given the poor quality of my hand and being out of position for the rest of the hand if I were to play, this is an easy fold.

K9 offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3x open from the Button, the best and safest play is to fold rather than call and play post-flop out of position with a easily dominated hand.
I have King of Spades and Queen of Spades on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take initiative.

Flash is opening extremely frequently from the button, indicating a very wide and likely weak range. Holding Jack-Ten suited in the Small Blind is a premium speculative hand. While calling is an option, 3-betting against such a wide opening range allows us to take the betting lead, apply pressure to force folds pre-flop, and play heads-up with a strong range advantage if called. I'll size up to 1000 to discourage flat calls and maximize fold equity out of position.
